'''Omatsu Daigongen''' (お松大権現) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamochō (Anan, Tokushima)|Kamochō]], [[:en:Anan, Tokushima|Anan City]], [[:en:Tokushima Prefecture|Tokushima Prefecture]]. It is managed by the religious corporation Omatsu Gongen-sha. Commonly referred to as the "Cat God Shrine" (猫神さん)<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8" />, it is one of the settings for Japan’s "Three Great Bakeneko Legends."
== History == [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,1.JPG|thumb|250px|Cat-shaped komainu]] [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,10.JPG|thumb|250px|Cherry blossoms in the shrine precincts]] [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,13.JPG|thumb|250px|Access road to the shrine]] [[File:Omatsu gongen Shrine office No,2.JPG|thumb|250px|Shop selling maneki-neko and omikuji]] During the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], in the [[Jōkyō era]] (1684–1688), a village headman in {{ill|Kamomura (Tokushima)|ja|加茂村 (徳島県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamomura (Tokushima)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day Kamochō, Anan City) borrowed money from a wealthy merchant to save the famine-stricken village. Although the debt was repaid, the merchant framed him for default. The headman, deeply distressed, fell ill and died. His land, which had been pledged as collateral, was seized by the merchant. The headman’s wife, Omatsu, petitioned the magistrate’s office but faced an unjust ruling due to the merchant’s bribery. When she appealed directly to the domain lord, Omatsu was executed for the act of direct petitioning. According to legend, her pet calico cat transformed into a [[:en:Bakeneko|Bakeneko]] (monster cat) and destroyed the homes of the merchant and the magistrate.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/yuisyo.html |title=Omatsu Gongen Shrine History |publisher=Omatsu Daigongen |accessdate=2017-01-01 |url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会">{{Cite web|url=http://www.anan-kankou.jp/bunya/omatudaigongen.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="情報サイト">{{Cite web|url=http://www.awanavi.jp/spot/2013032503003/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Awa Navi Tokushima Tourism Info |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref><ref name="阿南市">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.anan.tokushima.jp/docs/2010120700061/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref>
The exact cause of Omatsu’s death varies among legends. Some say only Omatsu was executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Awa’s Stubborn People |publisher=Awa Historical Fiction Society |date=2021 |page=190}}</ref> others suggest both Omatsu and her cat were executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Tales of Kamotani’s Past |editor=Kamotani Local History Research Association |page=37}}</ref> or that Omatsu committed suicide.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of Anan |publisher=Koyama Bookstore |date=1976 |page=56}}</ref>
=== Shrine Associated with Victory === Due to the legend of Omatsu's direct petitioning and subsequent defeat of evil, the shrine is believed to bring success in competitions. In recent years, this belief has extended to students praying for success in exams, particularly during the [[university entrance examination]] season. Amid [[Japan's Lost Decades]], the shrine has seen increased visits from job seekers and those praying for financial success. The shrine also accepts mail-in prayer requests for those unable to visit in person.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8">{{Cite web|url=http://www.topics.or.jp/localNews/news/2012/01/2012_13260017706.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen (Anan City) and Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City) Attract Students|publisher=Tokushima Shimbun|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会" /><ref name="情報サイト" /><ref name="阿南市" /> Uniquely, the shrine features cat-shaped [[:en:Komainu|Komainu]] (guardian statues).
== Timeline == * [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] – Shrine founded. * Early [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] – The enshrined deity's name was changed from Giri Daigongen to Omatsu Daigongen. * 1963 – Main shrine relocated to its current location.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Omatsu Daigongen: Cat God for Success and Victory |url=https://nekogami.jp/gosaijin.html |accessdate=2023-02-22}}</ref>
== Annual Events == {| class="wikitable" !Event<ref name="ご祭典">{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/saiten.html|title=Festivals|publisher=Omatsu Daigongen|accessdate=2017-1-1}}</ref>!!Date<ref name="ご祭典"/> |- |New Year Festival |January 1–3 |- |Omatsu's Memorial Day |March 15 (lunar calendar) |- |Grand Festival |May 1 |- |Monthly Festival |1st and 15th of every month |}
== Shrine Precincts == The precincts are adorned with cat-related objects and buildings. Over 10,000 maneki-neko (beckoning cat statues) have been dedicated and can be borrowed and taken home.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8"/><ref name="観光協会"/> Notable features include: * Maneki-neko * Prayer ema * Rubbing cat statues * Archives * Giant cat statue
<gallery> File:Omatsugongen Archives.JPG|Maneki-neko File:Omatsugongen prayer No,2.JPG|Prayer ema </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Anan, Tokushima|Anan City]] * {{ill|Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City)|ja|王子神社 (徳島市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City)|qq=}} – Also enshrines Omatsu and her beloved cat. * {{ill|Bakeneko#Saga of the Nabeshima Bakeneko|鍋島の化け猫騒動|lt=Nabeshima Bakeneko Incident|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakeneko#Saga of the Nabeshima Bakeneko|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoriyoshi Arima|ja|有馬頼義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoriyoshi Arima|1=Yorichika Arima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11516850}}

The headman’s wife, Omatsu, petitioned the magistrate’s office but faced an unjust ruling due to the merchant’s bribery. When she appealed directly to the domain lord, Omatsu was executed for the act of direct petitioning. According to legend, her pet calico cat transformed into a [[:en:Bakeneko|Bakeneko]] (monster cat) and destroyed the homes of the merchant and the magistrate.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/yuisyo.html |title=Omatsu Gongen Shrine History |publisher=Omatsu Daigongen |accessdate=2017-01-01 |url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会">{{Cite web|url=http://www.anan-kankou.jp/bunya/omatudaigongen.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="情報サイト">{{Cite web|url=http://www.awanavi.jp/spot/2013032503003/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Awa Navi Tokushima Tourism Info |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref><ref name="阿南市">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.anan.tokushima.jp/docs/2010120700061/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref>
The exact cause of Omatsu’s death varies among legends. Some say only Omatsu was executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Awa’s Stubborn People |publisher=Awa Historical Fiction Society |date=2021 |page=190}}</ref> others suggest both Omatsu and her cat were executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Tales of Kamotani’s Past |editor=Kamotani Local History Research Association |page=37}}</ref> or that Omatsu committed suicide.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of Anan |publisher=Koyama Bookstore |date=1976 |page=56}}</ref>
=== Shrine Associated with Victory === Due to the legend of Omatsu's direct petitioning and subsequent defeat of evil, the shrine is believed to bring success in competitions. In recent years, this belief has extended to students praying for success in exams, particularly during the [[university entrance examination]] season. Amid [[Japan's Lost Decades]], the shrine has seen increased visits from job seekers and those praying for financial success. The shrine also accepts mail-in prayer requests for those unable to visit in person.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8">{{Cite web|url=http://www.topics.or.jp/localNews/news/2012/01/2012_13260017706.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen (Anan City) and Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City) Attract Students|publisher=Tokushima Shimbun|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会" /><ref name="情報サイト" /><ref name="阿南市" /> Uniquely, the shrine features cat-shaped [[:en:Komainu|Komainu]] (guardian statues).
== Timeline == * [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] – Shrine founded. * Early [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] – The enshrined deity's name was changed from Giri Daigongen to Omatsu Daigongen. * 1963 – Main shrine relocated to its current location.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Omatsu Daigongen: Cat God for Success and Victory |url=https://nekogami.jp/gosaijin.html |accessdate=2023-02-22}}</ref>
== Annual Events == {| class="wikitable" !Event<ref name="ご祭典">{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/saiten.html|title=Festivals|publisher=Omatsu Daigongen|accessdate=2017-1-1}}</ref>!!Date<ref name="ご祭典"/> |- |New Year Festival |January 1–3 |- |Omatsu's Memorial Day |March 15 (lunar calendar) |- |Grand Festival |May 1 |- |Monthly Festival |1st and 15th of every month |}
== Shrine Precincts == The precincts are adorned with cat-related objects and buildings. Over 10,000 maneki-neko (beckoning cat statues) have been dedicated and can be borrowed and taken home.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8"/><ref name="観光協会"/> Notable features include: * Maneki-neko * Prayer ema * Rubbing cat statues * Archives * Giant cat statue
<gallery> File:Omatsugongen Archives.JPG|Maneki-neko File:Omatsugongen prayer No,2.JPG|Prayer ema </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Anan, Tokushima|Anan City]] * {{ill|Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City)|ja|王子神社 (徳島市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City)|qq=}} – Also enshrines Omatsu and her beloved cat. * {{ill|Bakeneko#Saga of the Nabeshima Bakeneko|鍋島の化け猫騒動|lt=Nabeshima Bakeneko Incident|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakeneko#Saga of the Nabeshima Bakeneko|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoriyoshi Arima|ja|有馬頼義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoriyoshi Arima|1=Yorichika Arima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11516850}}

'''Omatsu Daigongen''' (お松大権現) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamochō (Anan, Tokushima)|Kamochō]], [[:en:Anan, Tokushima|Anan City]], [[:en:Tokushima Prefecture|Tokushima Prefecture]]. It is managed by the religious corporation Omatsu Gongen-sha. Commonly referred to as the "Cat God Shrine" (猫神さん)<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8" />, it is one of the settings for Japan’s "Three Great Bakeneko Legends."
== History == [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,1.JPG|thumb|250px|Cat-shaped komainu]] [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,10.JPG|thumb|250px|Cherry blossoms in the shrine precincts]] [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,13.JPG|thumb|250px|Access road to the shrine]] [[File:Omatsu gongen Shrine office No,2.JPG|thumb|250px|Shop selling maneki-neko and omikuji]] During the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], in the [[Jōkyō era]] (1684–1688), a village headman in {{ill|Kamomura (Tokushima)|ja|加茂村 (徳島県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamomura (Tokushima)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day Kamochō, Anan City) borrowed money from a wealthy merchant to save the famine-stricken village. Although the debt was repaid, the merchant framed him for default. The headman, deeply distressed, fell ill and died. His land, which had been pledged as collateral, was seized by the merchant. The headman’s wife, Omatsu, petitioned the magistrate’s office but faced an unjust ruling due to the merchant’s bribery. When she appealed directly to the domain lord, Omatsu was executed for the act of direct petitioning. According to legend, her pet calico cat transformed into a [[:en:Bakeneko|Bakeneko]] (monster cat) and destroyed the homes of the merchant and the magistrate.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/yuisyo.html |title=Omatsu Gongen Shrine History |publisher=Omatsu Daigongen |accessdate=2017-01-01 |url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会">{{Cite web|url=http://www.anan-kankou.jp/bunya/omatudaigongen.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="情報サイト">{{Cite web|url=http://www.awanavi.jp/spot/2013032503003/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Awa Navi Tokushima Tourism Info |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref><ref name="阿南市">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.anan.tokushima.jp/docs/2010120700061/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref>
The exact cause of Omatsu’s death varies among legends. Some say only Omatsu was executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Awa’s Stubborn People |publisher=Awa Historical Fiction Society |date=2021 |page=190}}</ref> others suggest both Omatsu and her cat were executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Tales of Kamotani’s Past |editor=Kamotani Local History Research Association |page=37}}</ref> or that Omatsu committed suicide.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of Anan |publisher=Koyama Bookstore |date=1976 |page=56}}</ref>
=== Shrine Associated with Victory === Due to the legend of Omatsu's direct petitioning and subsequent defeat of evil, the shrine is believed to bring success in competitions. In recent years, this belief has extended to students praying for success in exams, particularly during the [[university entrance examination]] season. Amid [[Japan's Lost Decades]], the shrine has seen increased visits from job seekers and those praying for financial success. The shrine also accepts mail-in prayer requests for those unable to visit in person.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8">{{Cite web|url=http://www.topics.or.jp/localNews/news/2012/01/2012_13260017706.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen (Anan City) and Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City) Attract Students|publisher=Tokushima Shimbun|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会" /><ref name="情報サイト" /><ref name="阿南市" /> Uniquely, the shrine features cat-shaped [[:en:Komainu|Komainu]] (guardian statues).
== Timeline == * [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] – Shrine founded. * Early [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] – The enshrined deity's name was changed from Giri Daigongen to Omatsu Daigongen. * 1963 – Main shrine relocated to its current location.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Omatsu Daigongen: Cat God for Success and Victory |url=https://nekogami.jp/gosaijin.html |accessdate=2023-02-22}}</ref>
== Annual Events == {| class="wikitable" !Event<ref name="ご祭典">{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/saiten.html|title=Festivals|publisher=Omatsu Daigongen|accessdate=2017-1-1}}</ref>!!Date<ref name="ご祭典"/> |- |New Year Festival |January 1–3 |- |Omatsu's Memorial Day |March 15 (lunar calendar) |- |Grand Festival |May 1 |- |Monthly Festival |1st and 15th of every month |}
== Shrine Precincts == The precincts are adorned with cat-related objects and buildings. Over 10,000 maneki-neko (beckoning cat statues) have been dedicated and can be borrowed and taken home.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8"/><ref name="観光協会"/> Notable features include: * Maneki-neko * Prayer ema * Rubbing cat statues * Archives * Giant cat statue
<gallery> File:Omatsugongen Archives.JPG|Maneki-neko File:Omatsugongen prayer No,2.JPG|Prayer ema </gallery>

The headman’s wife, Omatsu, petitioned the magistrate’s office but faced an unjust ruling due to the merchant’s bribery. When she appealed directly to the domain lord, Omatsu was executed for the act of direct petitioning. According to legend, her pet calico cat transformed into a [[:en:Bakeneko|Bakeneko]] (monster cat) and destroyed the homes of the merchant and the magistrate.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/yuisyo.html |title=Omatsu Gongen Shrine History |publisher=Omatsu Daigongen |accessdate=2017-01-01 |url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会">{{Cite web|url=http://www.anan-kankou.jp/bunya/omatudaigongen.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="情報サイト">{{Cite web|url=http://www.awanavi.jp/spot/2013032503003/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Awa Navi Tokushima Tourism Info |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref><ref name="阿南市">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.anan.tokushima.jp/docs/2010120700061/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref>
The exact cause of Omatsu’s death varies among legends. Some say only Omatsu was executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Awa’s Stubborn People |publisher=Awa Historical Fiction Society |date=2021 |page=190}}</ref> others suggest both Omatsu and her cat were executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Tales of Kamotani’s Past |editor=Kamotani Local History Research Association |page=37}}</ref> or that Omatsu committed suicide.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of Anan |publisher=Koyama Bookstore |date=1976 |page=56}}</ref>
=== Shrine Associated with Victory === Due to the legend of Omatsu's direct petitioning and subsequent defeat of evil, the shrine is believed to bring success in competitions. In recent years, this belief has extended to students praying for success in exams, particularly during the [[university entrance examination]] season. Amid [[Japan's Lost Decades]], the shrine has seen increased visits from job seekers and those praying for financial success. The shrine also accepts mail-in prayer requests for those unable to visit in person.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8">{{Cite web|url=http://www.topics.or.jp/localNews/news/2012/01/2012_13260017706.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen (Anan City) and Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City) Attract Students|publisher=Tokushima Shimbun|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会" /><ref name="情報サイト" /><ref name="阿南市" /> Uniquely, the shrine features cat-shaped [[:en:Komainu|Komainu]] (guardian statues).
== Timeline == * [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] – Shrine founded. * Early [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] – The enshrined deity's name was changed from Giri Daigongen to Omatsu Daigongen. * 1963 – Main shrine relocated to its current location.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Omatsu Daigongen: Cat God for Success and Victory |url=https://nekogami.jp/gosaijin.html |accessdate=2023-02-22}}</ref>
== Annual Events == {| class="wikitable" !Event<ref name="ご祭典">{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/saiten.html|title=Festivals|publisher=Omatsu Daigongen|accessdate=2017-1-1}}</ref>!!Date<ref name="ご祭典"/> |- |New Year Festival |January 1–3 |- |Omatsu's Memorial Day |March 15 (lunar calendar) |- |Grand Festival |May 1 |- |Monthly Festival |1st and 15th of every month |}
== Shrine Precincts == The precincts are adorned with cat-related objects and buildings. Over 10,000 maneki-neko (beckoning cat statues) have been dedicated and can be borrowed and taken home.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8"/><ref name="観光協会"/> Notable features include: * Maneki-neko * Prayer ema * Rubbing cat statues * Archives * Giant cat statue
<gallery> File:Omatsugongen Archives.JPG|Maneki-neko File:Omatsugongen prayer No,2.JPG|Prayer ema </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Anan, Tokushima|Anan City]] * {{ill|Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City)|ja|王子神社 (徳島市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City)|qq=}} – Also enshrines Omatsu and her beloved cat. * {{ill|Bakeneko#Saga of the Nabeshima Bakeneko|鍋島の化け猫騒動|lt=Nabeshima Bakeneko Incident|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakeneko#Saga of the Nabeshima Bakeneko|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoriyoshi Arima|ja|有馬頼義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoriyoshi Arima|1=Yorichika Arima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11516850}}
== External Links == {{commonscat|Omatsugongen}} * [https://nekogami.jp/ Omatsu Daigongen Official Website]

'''Omatsu Daigongen''' (お松大権現) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamochō (Anan, Tokushima)|Kamochō]], [[:en:Anan, Tokushima|Anan City]], [[:en:Tokushima Prefecture|Tokushima Prefecture]]. It is managed by the religious corporation Omatsu Gongen-sha. Commonly referred to as the "Cat God Shrine" (猫神さん)<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8" />, it is one of the settings for Japan’s "Three Great Bakeneko Legends."
== History == [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,1.JPG|thumb|250px|Cat-shaped komainu]] [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,10.JPG|thumb|250px|Cherry blossoms in the shrine precincts]] [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,13.JPG|thumb|250px|Access road to the shrine]] [[File:Omatsu gongen Shrine office No,2.JPG|thumb|250px|Shop selling maneki-neko and omikuji]] During the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], in the [[Jōkyō era]] (1684–1688), a village headman in {{ill|Kamomura (Tokushima)|ja|加茂村 (徳島県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamomura (Tokushima)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day Kamochō, Anan City) borrowed money from a wealthy merchant to save the famine-stricken village. Although the debt was repaid, the merchant framed him for default. The headman, deeply distressed, fell ill and died. His land, which had been pledged as collateral, was seized by the merchant. The headman’s wife, Omatsu, petitioned the magistrate’s office but faced an unjust ruling due to the merchant’s bribery. When she appealed directly to the domain lord, Omatsu was executed for the act of direct petitioning. According to legend, her pet calico cat transformed into a [[:en:Bakeneko|Bakeneko]] (monster cat) and destroyed the homes of the merchant and the magistrate.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/yuisyo.html |title=Omatsu Gongen Shrine History |publisher=Omatsu Daigongen |accessdate=2017-01-01 |url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会">{{Cite web|url=http://www.anan-kankou.jp/bunya/omatudaigongen.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="情報サイト">{{Cite web|url=http://www.awanavi.jp/spot/2013032503003/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Awa Navi Tokushima Tourism Info |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref><ref name="阿南市">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.anan.tokushima.jp/docs/2010120700061/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref>
The exact cause of Omatsu’s death varies among legends. Some say only Omatsu was executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Awa’s Stubborn People |publisher=Awa Historical Fiction Society |date=2021 |page=190}}</ref> others suggest both Omatsu and her cat were executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Tales of Kamotani’s Past |editor=Kamotani Local History Research Association |page=37}}</ref> or that Omatsu committed suicide.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of Anan |publisher=Koyama Bookstore |date=1976 |page=56}}</ref>
=== Shrine Associated with Victory === Due to the legend of Omatsu's direct petitioning and subsequent defeat of evil, the shrine is believed to bring success in competitions. In recent years, this belief has extended to students praying for success in exams, particularly during the [[university entrance examination]] season. Amid [[Japan's Lost Decades]], the shrine has seen increased visits from job seekers and those praying for financial success. The shrine also accepts mail-in prayer requests for those unable to visit in person.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8">{{Cite web|url=http://www.topics.or.jp/localNews/news/2012/01/2012_13260017706.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen (Anan City) and Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City) Attract Students|publisher=Tokushima Shimbun|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会" /><ref name="情報サイト" /><ref name="阿南市" /> Uniquely, the shrine features cat-shaped [[:en:Komainu|Komainu]] (guardian statues).
== Timeline == * [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] – Shrine founded. * Early [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]] – The enshrined deity's name was changed from Giri Daigongen to Omatsu Daigongen. * 1963 – Main shrine relocated to its current location.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Omatsu Daigongen: Cat God for Success and Victory |url=https://nekogami.jp/gosaijin.html |accessdate=2023-02-22}}</ref>
== Annual Events == {| class="wikitable" !Event<ref name="ご祭典">{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/saiten.html|title=Festivals|publisher=Omatsu Daigongen|accessdate=2017-1-1}}</ref>!!Date<ref name="ご祭典"/> |- |New Year Festival |January 1–3 |- |Omatsu's Memorial Day |March 15 (lunar calendar) |- |Grand Festival |May 1 |- |Monthly Festival |1st and 15th of every month |}
== Shrine Precincts == The precincts are adorned with cat-related objects and buildings. Over 10,000 maneki-neko (beckoning cat statues) have been dedicated and can be borrowed and taken home.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8"/><ref name="観光協会"/> Notable features include: * Maneki-neko * Prayer ema * Rubbing cat statues * Archives * Giant cat statue
<gallery> File:Omatsugongen Archives.JPG|Maneki-neko File:Omatsugongen prayer No,2.JPG|Prayer ema </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Anan, Tokushima|Anan City]] * {{ill|Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City)|ja|王子神社 (徳島市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City)|qq=}} – Also enshrines Omatsu and her beloved cat. * {{ill|Bakeneko#Saga of the Nabeshima Bakeneko|鍋島の化け猫騒動|lt=Nabeshima Bakeneko Incident|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakeneko#Saga of the Nabeshima Bakeneko|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoriyoshi Arima|ja|有馬頼義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoriyoshi Arima|1=Yorichika Arima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11516850}}

Although the debt was repaid, the merchant framed him for default. The headman, deeply distressed, fell ill and died. His land, which had been pledged as collateral, was seized by the merchant. The headman’s wife, Omatsu, petitioned the magistrate’s office but faced an unjust ruling due to the merchant’s bribery. When she appealed directly to the domain lord, Omatsu was executed for the act of direct petitioning. According to legend, her pet calico cat transformed into a {{ill|Bakeneko|en|Bakeneko|WD=Q804321|lt=Bakeneko}} (monster cat) and destroyed the homes of the merchant and the magistrate.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/yuisyo.html |title=Omatsu Gongen Shrine History |publisher=Omatsu Daigongen |accessdate=2017-01-01 |url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会">{{Cite web|url=http://www.anan-kankou.jp/bunya/omatudaigongen.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="情報サイト">{{Cite web|url=http://www.awanavi.jp/spot/2013032503003/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Awa Navi Tokushima Tourism Info |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref><ref name="阿南市">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.anan.tokushima.jp/docs/2010120700061/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref>
The exact cause of Omatsu’s death varies among legends. Some say only Omatsu was executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Awa’s Stubborn People |publisher=Awa Historical Fiction Society |date=2021 |page=190}}</ref> others suggest both Omatsu and her cat were executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Tales of Kamotani’s Past |editor=Kamotani Local History Research Association |page=37}}</ref> or that Omatsu committed suicide.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of Anan |publisher=Koyama Bookstore |date=1976 |page=56}}</ref>
=== Shrine Associated with Victory === Due to the legend of Omatsu's direct petitioning and subsequent defeat of evil, the shrine is believed to bring success in competitions. In recent years, this belief has extended to students praying for success in exams, particularly during the [[university entrance examination]] season. Amid [[Japan's Lost Decades]], the shrine has seen increased visits from job seekers and those praying for financial success. The shrine also accepts mail-in prayer requests for those unable to visit in person.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8">{{Cite web|url=http://www.topics.or.jp/localNews/news/2012/01/2012_13260017706.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen (Anan City) and Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City) Attract Students|publisher=Tokushima Shimbun|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会" /><ref name="情報サイト" /><ref name="阿南市" /> Uniquely, the shrine features cat-shaped {{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu}} (guardian statues).
== Timeline == * {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} – Shrine founded. * Early {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa era}} – The enshrined deity's name was changed from Giri Daigongen to Omatsu Daigongen. * 1963 – Main shrine relocated to its current location.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Omatsu Daigongen: Cat God for Success and Victory |url=https://nekogami.jp/gosaijin.html |accessdate=2023-02-22}}</ref>
== Annual Events == {| class="wikitable" !Event<ref name="ご祭典">{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/saiten.html|title=Festivals|publisher=Omatsu Daigongen|accessdate=2017-1-1}}</ref>!!Date<ref name="ご祭典"/> |- |New Year Festival |January 1–3 |- |Omatsu's Memorial Day |March 15 (lunar calendar) |- |Grand Festival |May 1 |- |Monthly Festival |1st and 15th of every month |}
== Shrine Precincts == The precincts are adorned with cat-related objects and buildings. Over 10,000 maneki-neko (beckoning cat statues) have been dedicated and can be borrowed and taken home.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8"/><ref name="観光協会"/> Notable features include: * Maneki-neko * Prayer ema * Rubbing cat statues * Archives * Giant cat statue
<gallery> File:Omatsugongen Archives.JPG|Maneki-neko File:Omatsugongen prayer No,2.JPG|Prayer ema </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Anan, Tokushima|en|Anan, Tokushima|WD=Q485583|lt=Anan City}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City)|ja|王子神社 (徳島市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City)|qq=}} – Also enshrines Omatsu and her beloved cat. * {{ill|Bakeneko#Saga of the Nabeshima Bakeneko|鍋島の化け猫騒動|lt=Nabeshima Bakeneko Incident|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakeneko#Saga of the Nabeshima Bakeneko|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoriyoshi Arima|ja|有馬頼義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoriyoshi Arima|1=Yorichika Arima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11516850}}
== External Links == {{commonscat|Omatsugongen}} * [https://nekogami.jp/ Omatsu Daigongen Official Website]

'''Omatsu Daigongen''' (お松大権現) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in [[Kamochō (Anan, Tokushima)|Kamochō]], {{ill|Anan, Tokushima|en|Anan, Tokushima|WD=Q485583|lt=Anan City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}. It is managed by the religious corporation Omatsu Gongen-sha. Commonly referred to as the "Cat God Shrine" (猫神さん)<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8" />, it is one of the settings for Japan’s "Three Great Bakeneko Legends."
== History == [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,1.JPG|thumb|250px|Cat-shaped komainu]] [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,10.JPG|thumb|250px|Cherry blossoms in the shrine precincts]] [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,13.JPG|thumb|250px|Access road to the shrine]] [[File:Omatsu gongen Shrine office No,2.JPG|thumb|250px|Shop selling maneki-neko and omikuji]] During the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, in the [[Jōkyō era]] (1684–1688), a village headman in {{ill|Kamomura (Tokushima)|ja|加茂村 (徳島県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamomura (Tokushima)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day Kamochō, Anan City) borrowed money from a wealthy merchant to save the famine-stricken village. Although the debt was repaid, the merchant framed him for default. The headman, deeply distressed, fell ill and died. His land, which had been pledged as collateral, was seized by the merchant. The headman’s wife, Omatsu, petitioned the magistrate’s office but faced an unjust ruling due to the merchant’s bribery. When she appealed directly to the domain lord, Omatsu was executed for the act of direct petitioning. According to legend, her pet calico cat transformed into a {{ill|Bakeneko|en|Bakeneko|WD=Q804321|lt=Bakeneko}} (monster cat) and destroyed the homes of the merchant and the magistrate.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/yuisyo.html |title=Omatsu Gongen Shrine History |publisher=Omatsu Daigongen |accessdate=2017-01-01 |url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会">{{Cite web|url=http://www.anan-kankou.jp/bunya/omatudaigongen.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="情報サイト">{{Cite web|url=http://www.awanavi.jp/spot/2013032503003/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Awa Navi Tokushima Tourism Info |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref><ref name="阿南市">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.anan.tokushima.jp/docs/2010120700061/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref>
The exact cause of Omatsu’s death varies among legends. Some say only Omatsu was executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Awa’s Stubborn People |publisher=Awa Historical Fiction Society |date=2021 |page=190}}</ref> others suggest both Omatsu and her cat were executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Tales of Kamotani’s Past |editor=Kamotani Local History Research Association |page=37}}</ref> or that Omatsu committed suicide.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of Anan |publisher=Koyama Bookstore |date=1976 |page=56}}</ref>
=== Shrine Associated with Victory === Due to the legend of Omatsu's direct petitioning and subsequent defeat of evil, the shrine is believed to bring success in competitions. In recent years, this belief has extended to students praying for success in exams, particularly during the [[university entrance examination]] season. Amid [[Japan's Lost Decades]], the shrine has seen increased visits from job seekers and those praying for financial success. The shrine also accepts mail-in prayer requests for those unable to visit in person.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8">{{Cite web|url=http://www.topics.or.jp/localNews/news/2012/01/2012_13260017706.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen (Anan City) and Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City) Attract Students|publisher=Tokushima Shimbun|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会" /><ref name="情報サイト" /><ref name="阿南市" /> Uniquely, the shrine features cat-shaped {{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu}} (guardian statues).
== Timeline == * {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} – Shrine founded. * Early {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa era}} – The enshrined deity's name was changed from Giri Daigongen to Omatsu Daigongen. * 1963 – Main shrine relocated to its current location.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Omatsu Daigongen: Cat God for Success and Victory |url=https://nekogami.jp/gosaijin.html |accessdate=2023-02-22}}</ref>
== Annual Events == {| class="wikitable" !Event<ref name="ご祭典">{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/saiten.html|title=Festivals|publisher=Omatsu Daigongen|accessdate=2017-1-1}}</ref>!!Date<ref name="ご祭典"/> |- |New Year Festival |January 1–3 |- |Omatsu's Memorial Day |March 15 (lunar calendar) |- |Grand Festival |May 1 |- |Monthly Festival |1st and 15th of every month |}
== Shrine Precincts == The precincts are adorned with cat-related objects and buildings. Over 10,000 maneki-neko (beckoning cat statues) have been dedicated and can be borrowed and taken home.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8"/><ref name="観光協会"/> Notable features include: * Maneki-neko * Prayer ema * Rubbing cat statues * Archives * Giant cat statue

'''Omatsu Daigongen''' (お松大権現) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in [[Kamochō (Anan, Tokushima)|Kamochō]], {{ill|Anan, Tokushima|en|Anan, Tokushima|WD=Q485583|lt=Anan City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}. It is managed by the religious corporation Omatsu Gongen-sha. Commonly referred to as the "Cat God Shrine" (猫神さん)<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8" />, it is one of the settings for Japan’s "Three Great Bakeneko Legends."
== History == [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,1.JPG|thumb|250px|Cat-shaped komainu]] [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,10.JPG|thumb|250px|Cherry blossoms in the shrine precincts]] [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,13.JPG|thumb|250px|Access road to the shrine]] [[File:Omatsu gongen Shrine office No,2.JPG|thumb|250px|Shop selling maneki-neko and omikuji]] During the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, in the [[Jōkyō era]] (1684–1688), a village headman in {{ill|Kamomura (Tokushima)|ja|加茂村 (徳島県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamomura (Tokushima)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day Kamochō, Anan City) borrowed money from a wealthy merchant to save the famine-stricken village. Although the debt was repaid, the merchant framed him for default. The headman, deeply distressed, fell ill and died. His land, which had been pledged as collateral, was seized by the merchant. The headman’s wife, Omatsu, petitioned the magistrate’s office but faced an unjust ruling due to the merchant’s bribery. When she appealed directly to the domain lord, Omatsu was executed for the act of direct petitioning. According to legend, her pet calico cat transformed into a {{ill|Bakeneko|en|Bakeneko|WD=Q804321|lt=Bakeneko}} (monster cat) and destroyed the homes of the merchant and the magistrate.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/yuisyo.html |title=Omatsu Gongen Shrine History |publisher=Omatsu Daigongen |accessdate=2017-01-01 |url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会">{{Cite web|url=http://www.anan-kankou.jp/bunya/omatudaigongen.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="情報サイト">{{Cite web|url=http://www.awanavi.jp/spot/2013032503003/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Awa Navi Tokushima Tourism Info |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref><ref name="阿南市">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.anan.tokushima.jp/docs/2010120700061/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref>
The exact cause of Omatsu’s death varies among legends. Some say only Omatsu was executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Awa’s Stubborn People |publisher=Awa Historical Fiction Society |date=2021 |page=190}}</ref> others suggest both Omatsu and her cat were executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Tales of Kamotani’s Past |editor=Kamotani Local History Research Association |page=37}}</ref> or that Omatsu committed suicide.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of Anan |publisher=Koyama Bookstore |date=1976 |page=56}}</ref>
=== Shrine Associated with Victory === Due to the legend of Omatsu's direct petitioning and subsequent defeat of evil, the shrine is believed to bring success in competitions. In recent years, this belief has extended to students praying for success in exams, particularly during the [[university entrance examination]] season. Amid [[Japan's Lost Decades]], the shrine has seen increased visits from job seekers and those praying for financial success. The shrine also accepts mail-in prayer requests for those unable to visit in person.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8">{{Cite web|url=http://www.topics.or.jp/localNews/news/2012/01/2012_13260017706.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen (Anan City) and Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City) Attract Students|publisher=Tokushima Shimbun|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会" /><ref name="情報サイト" /><ref name="阿南市" /> Uniquely, the shrine features cat-shaped {{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu}} (guardian statues).
== Timeline == * {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} – Shrine founded. * Early {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa era}} – The enshrined deity's name was changed from Giri Daigongen to Omatsu Daigongen. * 1963 – Main shrine relocated to its current location.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Omatsu Daigongen: Cat God for Success and Victory |url=https://nekogami.jp/gosaijin.html |accessdate=2023-02-22}}</ref>
== Annual Events == {| class="wikitable" !Event<ref name="ご祭典">{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/saiten.html|title=Festivals|publisher=Omatsu Daigongen|accessdate=2017-1-1}}</ref>!!Date<ref name="ご祭典"/> |- |New Year Festival |January 1–3 |- |Omatsu's Memorial Day |March 15 (lunar calendar) |- |Grand Festival |May 1 |- |Monthly Festival |1st and 15th of every month |}
== Shrine Precincts == The precincts are adorned with cat-related objects and buildings. Over 10,000 maneki-neko (beckoning cat statues) have been dedicated and can be borrowed and taken home.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8"/><ref name="観光協会"/> Notable features include: * Maneki-neko * Prayer ema * Rubbing cat statues * Archives * Giant cat statue
<gallery> File:Omatsugongen Archives.JPG|Maneki-neko File:Omatsugongen prayer No,2.JPG|Prayer ema </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Anan, Tokushima|en|Anan, Tokushima|WD=Q485583|lt=Anan City}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City)|ja|王子神社 (徳島市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City)|qq=}} – Also enshrines Omatsu and her beloved cat. * {{ill|Bakeneko#Saga of the Nabeshima Bakeneko|鍋島の化け猫騒動|lt=Nabeshima Bakeneko Incident|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakeneko#Saga of the Nabeshima Bakeneko|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoriyoshi Arima|ja|有馬頼義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoriyoshi Arima|1=Yorichika Arima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11516850}}
== External Links == {{commonscat|Omatsugongen}} * [https://nekogami.jp/ Omatsu Daigongen Official Website]
{{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|Tokushima Prefecture}} {{translated page|ja|お松大権現|version=101558921|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Omatsu Daigongen | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Omatsugongen hall of worship.JPG |location = {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Anan, Tokushima|en|Anan, Tokushima|WD=Q485583|lt=Anan City|1=Anan, Tokushima}}, {{ill|Kamochō (Anan, Tokushima)|ja|加茂町 (阿南市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamochō (Anan, Tokushima)|1=Kamochō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398872}} 63 Fukuke |coordinates = {{Coord|33.914944|N|134.552722|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = (Honorary) Giri Daigongen (Omatsu, granddaughter of {{ill|Niki Yoshihiro|ja|仁木義広|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niki Yoshihiro|1=Nikki Yoshihiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24872537}}, daughter of Takusaimon) |SacredObject= Maki tree |ShrineRank = |Established = {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |Festival = |Rituals = |prefecture= {{ill|Anan|WD=Q485583|1=Anan, Tokushima}} }}
'''Omatsu Daigongen''' (お松大権現) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[Kamochō (Anan, Tokushima)|Kamochō]], {{ill|Anan, Tokushima|en|Anan, Tokushima|WD=Q485583|lt=Anan City|1=Anan, Tokushima}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}. It is managed by the religious corporation Omatsu Gongen-sha. Commonly referred to as the "Cat God Shrine" (猫神さん)<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8" />, it is one of the settings for Japan’s "Three Great Bakeneko Legends."
== History == [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,1.JPG|thumb|250px|Cat-shaped komainu]] [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,10.JPG|thumb|250px|Cherry blossoms in the shrine precincts]] [[File:Omatsugongen in 2014-4-4 No,13.JPG|thumb|250px|Access road to the shrine]] [[File:Omatsu gongen Shrine office No,2.JPG|thumb|250px|Shop selling maneki-neko and omikuji]] During the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, in the [[Jōkyō era]] (1684–1688), a village headman in {{ill|Kamomura (Tokushima)|ja|加茂村 (徳島県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamomura (Tokushima)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (modern-day Kamochō, Anan City) borrowed money from a wealthy merchant to save the famine-stricken village. Although the debt was repaid, the merchant framed him for default. The headman, deeply distressed, fell ill and died. His land, which had been pledged as collateral, was seized by the merchant. The headman’s wife, Omatsu, petitioned the magistrate’s office but faced an unjust ruling due to the merchant’s bribery. When she appealed directly to the domain lord, Omatsu was executed for the act of direct petitioning. According to legend, her pet calico cat transformed into a {{ill|Bakeneko|en|Bakeneko|WD=Q804321|lt=Bakeneko|1=Bakeneko}} (monster cat) and destroyed the homes of the merchant and the magistrate.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/yuisyo.html |title=Omatsu Gongen Shrine History |publisher=Omatsu Daigongen |accessdate=2017-01-01 |url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会">{{Cite web|url=http://www.anan-kankou.jp/bunya/omatudaigongen.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City Tourism Association|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="情報サイト">{{Cite web|url=http://www.awanavi.jp/spot/2013032503003/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Awa Navi Tokushima Tourism Info |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref><ref name="阿南市">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.anan.tokushima.jp/docs/2010120700061/|title=Omatsu Daigongen|publisher=Anan City |accessdate=2014-4-5}}</ref>
The exact cause of Omatsu’s death varies among legends. Some say only Omatsu was executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Awa’s Stubborn People |publisher=Awa Historical Fiction Society |date=2021 |page=190}}</ref> others suggest both Omatsu and her cat were executed,<ref>{{Cite book|title=Tales of Kamotani’s Past |editor=Kamotani Local History Research Association |page=37}}</ref> or that Omatsu committed suicide.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of Anan |publisher=Koyama Bookstore |date=1976 |page=56}}</ref>
=== Shrine Associated with Victory === Due to the legend of Omatsu's direct petitioning and subsequent defeat of evil, the shrine is believed to bring success in competitions. In recent years, this belief has extended to students praying for success in exams, particularly during the [[university entrance examination]] season. Amid [[Japan's Lost Decades]], the shrine has seen increased visits from job seekers and those praying for financial success. The shrine also accepts mail-in prayer requests for those unable to visit in person.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8">{{Cite web|url=http://www.topics.or.jp/localNews/news/2012/01/2012_13260017706.html|title=Omatsu Daigongen (Anan City) and Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City) Attract Students|publisher=Tokushima Shimbun|accessdate=2014-04-05|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="観光協会" /><ref name="情報サイト" /><ref name="阿南市" /> Uniquely, the shrine features cat-shaped {{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu|1=Komainu}} (guardian statues).
== Timeline == * {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} – Shrine founded. * Early {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa era|1=Shōwa era}} – The enshrined deity's name was changed from Giri Daigongen to Omatsu Daigongen. * 1963 – Main shrine relocated to its current location.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Omatsu Daigongen: Cat God for Success and Victory |url=https://nekogami.jp/gosaijin.html |accessdate=2023-02-22}}</ref>
== Annual Events == {| class="wikitable" !Event<ref name="ご祭典">{{Cite web|url=http://wwwa.pikara.ne.jp/omatudaigongen/saiten.html|title=Festivals|publisher=Omatsu Daigongen|accessdate=2017-1-1}}</ref>!!Date<ref name="ご祭典"/> |- |New Year Festival |January 1–3 |- |Omatsu's Memorial Day |March 15 (lunar calendar) |- |Grand Festival |May 1 |- |Monthly Festival |1st and 15th of every month |}
== Shrine Precincts == The precincts are adorned with cat-related objects and buildings. Over 10,000 maneki-neko (beckoning cat statues) have been dedicated and can be borrowed and taken home.<ref name="徳島新聞 2012.1.8"/><ref name="観光協会"/> Notable features include: * Maneki-neko * Prayer ema * Rubbing cat statues * Archives * Giant cat statue
<gallery> File:Omatsugongen Archives.JPG|Maneki-neko File:Omatsugongen prayer No,2.JPG|Prayer ema </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Anan, Tokushima|en|Anan, Tokushima|WD=Q485583|lt=Anan City|1=Anan, Tokushima}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City)|ja|王子神社 (徳島市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine (Tokushima City)|qq=}} – Also enshrines Omatsu and her beloved cat. * {{ill|Bakeneko#Saga of the Nabeshima Bakeneko|鍋島の化け猫騒動|lt=Nabeshima Bakeneko Incident|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakeneko#Saga of the Nabeshima Bakeneko|qq=}} * {{ill|Yoriyoshi Arima|ja|有馬頼義|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoriyoshi Arima|1=Yorichika Arima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11516850}}
== External Links == {{commonscat|Omatsugongen}} * [https://nekogami.jp/ Omatsu Daigongen Official Website]
{{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|Tokushima Prefecture}} {{translated page|ja|お松大権現|version=101558921|comment=Imported full ja history}}
